retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pituitary Gland -- Clinical Significance -- Acromegaly and Gigantism
retrieved_docs.content: Excess of GH after epiphyseal closure causes acromegaly whereas gigantism results from GH hypersecretion before epiphyseal closure. Acromegaly occurs more frequently than gigantism. Causes: pituitary adenomas composed of somatotrophs.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Growth Hormone -- Clinical Significance -- Gigantism
retrieved_docs.content: This illness is very similar to acromegaly in all aspects, except the underlying pituitary adenoma develops before the closure of long bone epiphysis. Therefore, bone growth occurs in long bones such as the tibia, fibula, femur, humerus, radius, and ulna. Since epiphyseal closure occurs before adulthood, this is typically an illness with an onset seen in children. The organ and metabolic impacts are similar to acromegaly.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Hypothalamus -- Clinical Significance -- Acromegaly and Pituitary Gigantism
retrieved_docs.content: Adolescents and children with pituitary gigantism present most often with a rapid abnormal increase in height concurrent with rapid weight gain. [10] Other less common features include large hands and feet, macrocephaly, coarsening of the facial features, and excessive sweating. Adults with acromegaly present with soft tissue overgrowth and skin thickening, with the characteristic features of macrognathia, macroglossia, and enlarged hands and feet, hypertrophy of the knees, ankles, hips, and spine, visceral enlargement of the thyroid and heart, insulin resistance, and diabetes. In particular, adults with growth hormone excess do not present with an increase in height due to the growth hormone excess occurring after the fusion of the epiphyseal growth plates. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Gigantism and Acromegaly -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Acromegaly and gigantism are disorders of growth hormone hypersecretion. The most common cause is a growth hormone (GH) secreting adenoma in the pituitary gland. Gigantism occurs when growth hormone hypersecretion occurs before the fusion of the long bone epiphysis and is characterized by tall stature. Acromegaly occurs when GH hypersecretion occurs after the fusion of the epiphysis leading to large extremities and characteristic facies. An elevated insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) level establishes the diagnosis. The first line of treatment is surgical excision of the tumor; however, this rarely results in a cure, and further medical treatment with somatostatin analogs or radiation is usually necessary. This activity explains the evaluation and management of gigantism and acromegaly and emphasizes the role of the interprofessional team in improving the care for patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Gigantism and Acromegaly -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Acromegaly and gigantism are disorders of growth hormone hypersecretion (see Image. Acromegaly, Gigantism). The most common cause is a growth hormone (GH) secreting adenoma in the pituitary gland. Gigantism occurs when growth hormone hypersecretion occurs before the fusion of the long bone epiphysis and is characterized by tall stature. Acromegaly occurs when GH hypersecretion occurs after the fusion of the epiphysis leading to large extremities and characteristic facies. An elevated insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) level establishes the diagnosis. The first line of treatment is surgical excision of the tumor; however, this rarely results in a cure, and further medical treatment with somatostatin analogs or radiation is usually necessary. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Hypothalamus -- Clinical Significance -- Acromegaly and Pituitary Gigantism
retrieved_docs.content: Both acromegaly and pituitary gigantism are rare disorders of growth, occurring in anywhere between 40 to 125 per million people, that occur due to persistent secretion of growth hormone from the pituitary gland. Pituitary gigantism occurs in adolescents and children who have growth hormone excess before the fusion of their epiphyseal growth plates whereas acromegaly occurs in adults who have growth hormone excess after the fusion of their epiphyseal growth plates. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Gigantism and Acromegaly -- History and Physical -- Acromegaly Characteristics
retrieved_docs.content: Increased size of extremities: Enlargement of the hands and feet is noted secondary to both bony expansion and soft tissue swelling. Patients often appreciate an increase in ring and shoe size. The extremities have a "dough" like consistency due to the soft tissue swelling. There is a decrease in the shoe size and ring size with the treatment of acromegaly due to the resolution of soft tissue swelling; the bony changes, however, are permanent.

retrieved_docs.title: Gigantism and Acromegaly -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy involves either the use of external irradiation or stereotactic single high-dose irradiation, depending on the location and extent of residual disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pituitary Gland -- Clinical Significance -- Acromegaly and Gigantism
retrieved_docs.content: Pathophysiology: excess GH causes an increase in the growth of soft tissues and increases in IGF-1production from the liver, which promotes growth in the bones of the hands and feet. Individuals with acromegaly may also develop carbohydrate intolerance and diabetes due to excess GH.

retrieved_docs.title: Gigantism and Acromegaly -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory assessment includes measurement of IGF-1 level; normal value rules out acromegaly.

retrieved_docs.title: Gigantism and Acromegaly -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Acromegaly and gigantism are due to the oversecretion of growth hormone. The most common cause is a GH-secreting pituitary adenoma. Rarely, ectopic GH secretion or excess secretion of GHRH may be the cause.

retrieved_docs.title: Gigantism and Acromegaly -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Mortality is increased in untreated acromegaly, with cardiovascular disease being the major contributor, but the quality of life of these patients has generally improved in recent years. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Gigantism and Acromegaly -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Surgery is the preferred initial modality of treatment. Residual or persistent findings can be treated medically or with radiotherapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Gigantism and Acromegaly -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: If IGF-1 is high, GH levels are assessed before and after OGTT to confirm the diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Acromegaly -- History and Physical -- Limbs
retrieved_docs.content: Acral enlargement: large hands (with stubby fingers) and feet Proximal myopathy Carpal tunnel syndrome Genu varum Rolling gait [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Gigantism and Acromegaly -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Gigantism is characterized by tall stature and should be suspected in children three standard deviations above the mean. Acromegaly is characterized by large hands and feet, coarse facial features, a broad nose, acne, hyperhidrosis, underbite, and teeth separation. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1136
retrieved_docs.content: Arthropathy associated with an endocrine AND/OR metabolic disorder is a joint‑related disease that is identified in the klouby (joints). Surgical procedures described as incision and excision of joint structures also pertain to the klouby, indicating that both the pathological manifestation of endocrine‑ or metabolic‑related arthropathy and its operative management are centered on joint anatomy. This underscores the clinical relevance of recognizing endocrine or metabolic contributions to joint disease and the importance of joint‑focused surgical interventions in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 469
retrieved_docs.content: Arthropathy associated with an endocrine AND/OR metabolic disorder is classified under other non‑traumatic joint disorders. This arthropathy has its primary finding site in the joints (klouby) and is linked to the broader concept of joint disease (اعتلال مفصلي). Recognizing that this condition maps to other non‑traumatic joint disorders and involves the joints helps clinicians understand how endocrine or metabolic disturbances can produce non‑traumatic arthropathies that belong to the overall spectrum of joint disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 143
retrieved_docs.content: Arthropathy associated with an endocrine AND/OR metabolic disorder is classified as other non‑traumatic joint disorders and its primary finding site is the joints (klouby). Other non‑traumatic joint disorders are linked to the concept of اعتلال مفصلي (joint disease). In the context of surgical procedures, Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage is a subcategory (has parent) of Incision and excision of joint structures and it classifies the anatomical category “division of joint capsule; ligament or cartilage.” Incision and excision of joint structures also has a relationship with the joints (klouby). This integrated knowledge connects endocrine‑related arthropathy to non‑traumatic joint pathology, emphasizes its joint involvement, and outlines the hierarchical relationship between joint‑focused surgical interventions and the specific joint structures they address.
